intro.snapshot_description: Chapter 6 bridges kinematics and dynamics by introducing scalar energy quantities. Work (W = Fs cosθ) links force to displacement; the work-energy theorem ties net work to change in kinetic energy. Potential energy (gravitational mgh, elastic ½kx²) completes mechanical energy conservation. Power measures the rate of doing work (P = F·v). Collisions — elastic, inelastic, perfectly inelastic — are analysed via momentum conservation and the coefficient of restitution. Vertical circular motion unifies all concepts: minimum speed conditions at the top (√gr) and bottom (√5gr) of a loop govern tension and energy analysis.

expected_questions.description: NEET consistently places 3–4 questions from this chapter every year, often blending work-energy theorem with collision scenarios.

time_required.description: Allow ~3 hrs for work and energy formulas, ~3 hrs for collisions, ~2 hrs for power, and ~2 hrs for vertical circular motion with practice problems.

difficulty_level.description: Formula application is straightforward but NEET questions combine multiple concepts (e.g., energy conservation plus collision type plus vertical circle) in a single problem.

intro.most_asked_style: Numerical problems requiring application of work-energy theorem, elastic collision velocity formulas, and minimum speed in vertical circular motion — often with a twist on which bodies are stationary or moving in opposite directions.
intro.biggest_trap: Assuming kinetic energy is conserved in all collisions. Only perfectly elastic collisions conserve KE; perfectly inelastic (bodies stick) and plain inelastic do not. Also confusing W_spring = −½kx² (by spring on block) vs U_spring = +½kx² (stored in spring).
intro.fast_win: Memorise the five special-case elastic collision outcomes (equal masses exchange velocities; heavy hits stationary light → light moves at 2u; light hits heavy stationary → light bounces back). These appear almost every year.
intro.revision_friendly: Draw energy pie charts (KE ↔ PE) for conservation problems and a standard elastic collision table. Both fit on one page and recover 80% of marks in this chapter.

subtopics.subtitle: From W = Fs cosθ to collision formulas and vertical loops — master scalar energy methods
subtopics.revision_tip: Always start with energy conservation; switch to momentum conservation only when forces are impulsive (collision). For vertical circle, check both tension and velocity conditions at top and bottom simultaneously.

topics.topic_description: Defines work as the dot product of force and displacement (W = F·s cosθ), covers positive, negative and zero work, variable force work via integration, and proves the work-energy theorem W_net = ΔKE = ½mv² − ½mu².

subtopics.name: Constant Force Work
subtopics.description: W = Fs cosθ; positive (θ < 90°), negative (θ > 90°), zero (θ = 90° or s = 0 or F = 0).

subtopics.name: Variable Force Work
subtopics.description: W = ∫F·ds; area under F-x graph equals work done.

subtopics.name: Work-Energy Theorem
subtopics.description: W_net = ΔKE = ½mv² − ½mu²; valid for all force types, including non-conservative.

subtopics.name: Conservative vs Non-conservative Forces
subtopics.description: Conservative (gravity, spring): path-independent work, closed-loop work = 0. Non-conservative (friction): path-dependent, closed-loop work ≠ 0.

topics.topic_description: Covers kinetic energy KE = ½mv² = P²/2m, gravitational PE = mgh, elastic PE = ½kx², and the law of conservation of mechanical energy. Relates momentum and kinetic energy (P = √(2mE)).

subtopics.name: Kinetic Energy
subtopics.description: KE = ½mv²; always positive scalar; KE = P²/2m links momentum and energy.

subtopics.name: Gravitational Potential Energy
subtopics.description: U = mgh (near Earth surface); zero reference chosen at ground.

subtopics.name: Elastic (Spring) Potential Energy
subtopics.description: U = ½kx²; work done by spring on block = −½kx² (spring force opposes extension).

subtopics.name: Law of Conservation of Mechanical Energy
subtopics.description: KE + PE = constant in conservative fields; loss in KE = gain in PE and vice versa. With friction: ΔE = W_friction.

subtopics.name: Potential Energy Curves and Equilibrium
subtopics.description: F = −dU/dx; stable equilibrium at PE minimum (d²U/dx² > 0); unstable at maximum.

topics.topic_description: Power P = dW/dt = F·v (instantaneous) or P_avg = W/t. Collision types: elastic (e = 1, KE conserved), perfectly inelastic (e = 0, bodies merge), inelastic (0 < e < 1). Elastic 1D formulas: v1 = (m1−m2)u1/(m1+m2) + 2m2u2/(m1+m2); key special cases; loss in KE for perfectly inelastic = ½·m1m2/(m1+m2)·(u1−u2)².

subtopics.name: Power
subtopics.description: P_avg = W/t; P_inst = F·v; units: Watt, hp (1 hp = 746 W); kWh is energy unit.

subtopics.name: Coefficient of Restitution
subtopics.description: e = (v2 − v1)/(u1 − u2); elastic e=1; perfectly inelastic e=0; inelastic 0 < e < 1.

subtopics.name: Perfectly Elastic Head-on Collision
subtopics.description: KE conserved; velocities: v1=(m1−m2)u1/(m1+m2)+2m2u2/(m1+m2); v2=2m1u1/(m1+m2)+(m2−m1)u2/(m1+m2). Equal masses: exchange velocities.

subtopics.name: Rebounding Ball (e)
subtopics.description: After nth bounce: height hn = e²ⁿh0; total distance H = h0·(1+e²)/(1−e²); total time T = √(2h0/g)·(1+e)/(1−e).

topics.topic_description: Motion in a vertical loop: velocity and tension vary with position. Critical conditions: minimum speed at top v_C = √(gl) (tension = 0); minimum speed at bottom v_A = √(5gl) to complete loop; T_bottom − T_top = 6mg. Energy conservation connects all positions.

subtopics.name: Velocity at Any Point
subtopics.description: v = √(u² − 2gl(1 − cosθ)) where u is speed at lowest point and l is string length.

subtopics.name: Tension at Any Point
subtopics.description: T = (m/l)[u² − gl(2 − 3cosθ)]; at bottom T_A = mu²/l + mg; at top T_C = mu²/l − 5mg.

subtopics.name: Critical Condition
subtopics.description: For just completing the circle, T_C = 0 → u_min = √(5gl) at bottom; v_min = √(gl) at top.

subtopics.name: Tension Difference
subtopics.description: T_bottom − T_top = 6mg (independent of speed and radius at critical condition).

subtopics.name: Block on Frictionless Hemisphere
subtopics.description: Block loses contact at height h = 2r/3 (angle cosθ = 2/3) from ground.

topics.topic_description: Core concept linking force and displacement via scalar product; work-energy theorem is the fundamental tool for energy-based problem solving.
topics.notes_condensed_description: W = Fs cosθ (constant F); W = ∫F·ds (variable F) = area under F-x graph. Net work done = change in KE: W_net = ½mv² − ½mu². Conservative forces (gravity, spring): W independent of path, closed-loop W = 0. Non-conservative forces (friction): path-dependent, closed-loop W ≠ 0. Frame-dependent: work changes with frame.
topics.how_to_revise: Draw the F-x graph for spring and shade the triangular area. Verify work-energy theorem on 2-3 standard problems (inclined plane + friction). Practise closed-loop W = 0 proofs for gravity and spring.

expected_questions.description: Usually 1 direct question on W = Fs cosθ or work-energy theorem, often combined with an inclined plane or circular path.

time_required.description: Cover theory in 1 hr; solve 10 past NEET questions in 2 hrs.

difficulty.description: Formula application is easy; distinguishing conservative vs non-conservative contexts is where students lose marks.

topics.scoring_focus: Work-energy theorem applied to stopping distance (x = mv²/2F) and block-spring problems: these are direct 1-mark scorers.
topics.high_risk_area: Mixing up W_by_spring (negative: −½kx²) and U_spring (positive: +½kx²). Also forgetting that centripetal force does zero work.
topics.best_practice_style: Mixed numerical + conceptual MCQs; 60% computation, 40% identification of sign and conditions.
topics.priority_rule: Solve first: stopping distance, block on incline, spring compression. These three patterns cover ~70% of work-energy MCQs in NEET.

topics.topic_description: KE, gravitational PE, elastic PE and their interconversion under conservation of mechanical energy. Equilibrium analysis using PE curves.
topics.notes_condensed_description: KE = ½mv² = P²/2m; momentum P = √(2mE). PE_gravity = mgh; PE_spring = ½kx². Conservation: KE + PE = E (constant) when only conservative forces act. With friction: E_final = E_initial + W_friction. Stable equilibrium: PE minimum, d²U/dx² > 0. Unstable: PE maximum. Body loses contact with hemisphere surface at h = 2r/3.
topics.how_to_revise: Practise energy bar charts (KE, PE, total E) at three positions: start, midpoint, end. Memorise P = √(2mE) and apply to comparison problems. Sketch PE curves and identify equilibrium points.

expected_questions.description: One question typically tests conservation of energy in projectile, spring-block or roller-coaster context.

time_required.description: 1 hr theory + 2 hrs problem sets on spring-mass systems and PE curves.

difficulty.description: PE concepts are manageable; PE curve equilibrium analysis (stable/unstable/neutral) can trip students in assertion-reason format.

topics.scoring_focus: Solving for velocity after a spring releases a block (½kx² = ½mv²) or height reached after collision using conservation — direct 1-mark problems.
topics.high_risk_area: Sign error in work done by spring: W_by_spring = −½kx² (spring does negative work on block when block compresses or stretches it from mean). KE can never be negative.
topics.best_practice_style: Mostly numerical; assertion-reason for PE curves. Equal weight on spring-block and gravitational scenarios.
topics.priority_rule: Master spring-block energy conservation and KE-momentum relations before moving to PE curves.

topics.topic_description: Power as rate of work; elastic and inelastic collision analysis via momentum conservation plus coefficient of restitution framework.
topics.notes_condensed_description: P_avg = W/t; P_inst = F·v; 1 hp = 746 W; kWh is energy. Collision: total momentum conserved always; KE only in elastic. e = (v2−v1)/(u1−u2): elastic e=1, perfectly inelastic e=0. Elastic 1D: v1=(m1−m2)u1/(m1+m2)+2m2u2/(m1+m2); v2=2m1u1/(m1+m2)+(m2−m1)u2/(m1+m2). Equal masses: exchange velocities (v1=u2, v2=u1). Heavy hits light stationary: v1≈u1, v2=2u1. Light hits heavy stationary: v1≈−u1. Perfectly inelastic: v_comb=(m1u1+m2u2)/(m1+m2); ΔKE_loss = ½·m1m2/(m1+m2)·(u1−u2)². Bullet-block: v_block = mu/(m+M), then ½(m+M)v² = (m+M)gh gives h.
topics.how_to_revise: Write the three sets of elastic collision special cases from memory. Derive ΔKE_loss formula for perfectly inelastic once. Practice e = 0.5 inelastic problems. For power: solve 3 automobile-power problems using constant power equation v = √(2Pt/m).

expected_questions.description: Collisions get 1–2 questions most years; power occasionally appears as a standalone or embedded in a collision scenario.

time_required.description: 1 hr power + 3 hrs collision types with NEET past questions.

difficulty.description: Elastic collision velocity derivation and special-case memory require sustained practice; traps exist in sign conventions and direction assumptions.

topics.scoring_focus: Equal-mass elastic collision (exchange velocities) and bullet-block perfectly inelastic collision appear almost every NEET year — learn these cold.
topics.high_risk_area: Forgetting that KE is not conserved in 'inelastic' (most real-world) collisions. Also sign errors when one body moves opposite to the other before collision (negative initial velocity convention).
topics.best_practice_style: Mostly numerical with 4 options close in value, testing precision in v1/v2 formula. Conceptual MCQs on e=1 vs e=0 conditions.
topics.priority_rule: Perfect elastic 1D special cases → perfectly inelastic ΔKE formula → power P=F·v. This order maximises marks per hour.

topics.topic_description: Non-uniform circular motion under gravity; critical speed conditions, tension calculations, and energy distribution at every point of the loop.
topics.notes_condensed_description: For circular loop of radius l with body at lowest point A: v = √(u²−2gl(1−cosθ)). Critical condition (T_C = 0 at top): u_min = √(5gl); v at top = √(gl); T at bottom = 6mg; T at horizontal = 3mg. Difference: T_A − T_C = 6mg always. If √(2gl) < u < √(5gl): string slackens between horizontal and top (parabolic trajectory). If u < √(2gl): oscillates. Block on hemisphere loses contact at h = 2r/3 (cosθ = 2/3).
topics.how_to_revise: Draw the vertical loop, label positions A (bottom), B (side), C (top). Fill in Table 3.5 values from memory: velocities √(5gl), √(3gl), √(gl), tensions 6mg, 3mg, 0. Then derive T_A − T_C = 6mg from formulas.

expected_questions.description: One question on minimum speed or tension difference in vertical circle appears in most NEET exams.

time_required.description: 30 min theory + table memorisation; 1.5 hrs problem sets including NEET and AIIMS past questions.

difficulty.description: The five-case table (√5gl, between √2gl-√5gl, √2gl, below √2gl) and tension difference formula require careful memorisation; application is straightforward once memorised.

topics.scoring_focus: T_A − T_C = 6mg is a direct formula question scorer. Minimum speed to complete loop (√(5gl) at bottom) appears as a 1-mark direct question.
topics.high_risk_area: Confusing the speed at top (√gl) with speed at bottom (√5gl) — NEET distractors specifically exploit this. Also missing that the hemisphere detachment height is 2r/3, not r/2.
topics.best_practice_style: Direct formula application numericals (60%) and conceptual condition-based MCQs (40% — which regime does the particle fall into for a given u?).
topics.priority_rule: Memorise: v_bottom_min = √(5gl), v_top_min = √(gl), T_difference = 6mg. All three appear in NEET at least once every 2 years.

mistakes.label: Confusing stored energy with work by spring
mistakes.description: U_spring = +½kx² (energy stored in spring), but work done BY the spring on the block = −½kx² (spring force opposes displacement from natural length).

mistakes.label: Double-counting work
mistakes.description: Students add both external agent's work AND spring's work to get ΔKE, but they are equal and opposite — W_external = +½kx² = −W_spring.

topics.example: A spring (k = 200 N/m) is compressed by 0.1 m. A student claims work done by spring is +1 J. Correct: W_spring on block = −½ × 200 × 0.01 = −1 J (negative; spring pushes block in direction of release, which is opposite to compression).
topics.trap_framing: Work done by spring vs potential energy stored in spring

trap_question.stem: A spring of spring constant k is compressed by x from its natural length. The work done BY the spring on the block during this compression is:
trap_question.trick: Answer is −½kx² (B). The spring exerts a restoring force opposing the compression direction, so work done by the spring is negative. U = +½kx² is the elastic PE stored, not the work done by the spring.

topics.quick_rule: W_by_spring = −½kx² (always negative during compression or extension); U_spring = +½kx² (always positive, stored energy).

mistakes.label: Forgetting the heavy-hits-light formula
mistakes.description: When a very heavy body (m1 >> m2) hits a stationary light body: v1 ≈ u1 (heavy barely slows down) and v2 = 2u1 (light flies off at twice the speed). Students often write v2 = u1.

mistakes.label: Equal mass direction confusion
mistakes.description: If m1 = m2 and both are moving in the same direction, they exchange velocities. But if u2 = 0, then v1 = 0, v2 = u1 — the first body stops completely.

topics.example: A truck (mass 1000 kg, velocity 10 m/s) elastically hits a stationary ball (mass 1 kg). Students answer v_ball = 10 m/s. Correct: v_ball = 2 × 10 = 20 m/s (light body gets almost double the incoming speed).
topics.trap_framing: Special cases of elastic 1D collisions — NEET loves heavy-vs-light scenarios

trap_question.stem: A ball of mass m moving with speed u makes a perfectly elastic head-on collision with a stationary ball of mass 3m. The velocity of the lighter ball after collision is:
trap_question.trick: Use v1 = (m − 3m)/(m + 3m)·u = (−2m/4m)·u = −u/2. Answer is B (−u/2, backwards). When light hits heavy stationary body, the light ball bounces back.

topics.quick_rule: Light ball hits heavy stationary: v_light = −[(m2−m1)/(m1+m2)]u ≈ −u (bounces back). Heavy hits stationary light: v_light ≈ 2u (flies forward at nearly double speed).

mistakes.label: Swapping top and bottom speed
mistakes.description: v_min at top of loop = √(gl), NOT √(5gl). v_min at bottom = √(5gl) to just complete the circle. Students regularly interchange these.

mistakes.label: Missing T_bottom − T_top = 6mg
mistakes.description: This result is derived from energy conservation + tension formulas and is independent of speed. However, students recompute T at each point instead of using this elegant result.

topics.example: NEET 2018 asked: a body slides from height h to just complete a vertical circle of diameter D. Students chose h = D (wrong). Answer: h = 5D/4 from energy conservation (½mv² = mgh gives v² = √(5gl) at bottom, h = 5l/2 = 5D/4).
topics.trap_framing: Minimum speed and tension at top vs bottom of a vertical loop

trap_question.stem: A stone of mass m is tied to a string of length l and whirled in a vertical circle. The minimum velocity at the lowest point so that the string does not go slack at the highest point is:
trap_question.trick: Answer is D — √(5gl). The critical condition is T = 0 at top, giving v_top = √(gl). From energy conservation: v_bottom² = v_top² + 4gl = gl + 4gl = 5gl, so v_bottom = √(5gl).

topics.quick_rule: √(gl) = minimum speed at TOP; √(5gl) = minimum speed at BOTTOM; difference in tension T_bottom − T_top = 6mg.

mistakes.label: Assuming all collisions conserve KE
mistakes.description: Only perfectly elastic collisions (e = 1) conserve kinetic energy. ALL collisions conserve momentum.

mistakes.label: Confusing total energy with kinetic energy
mistakes.description: Total energy (including heat, deformation energy) is always conserved. KE is only conserved in elastic collisions.

topics.example: A bullet embeds in a wooden block — KE is NOT conserved (perfectly inelastic, e = 0). Students incorrectly use KE_before = KE_after. Use momentum conservation to find v_combined, then KE_after = ½(M+m)v_combined².
topics.trap_framing: Which quantity is conserved in elastic vs inelastic vs perfectly inelastic collisions?

trap_question.stem: In a perfectly inelastic collision between two bodies of equal mass m, one of which is at rest, the fraction of kinetic energy lost is:
trap_question.trick: Answer is C (1/2). By momentum conservation: mv = 2mv_f → v_f = v/2. KE_initial = ½mv²; KE_final = ½(2m)(v/2)² = mv²/4; ΔKE = ½mv²(1 − 1/2) = mv²/4. Fraction lost = (mv²/4)/(mv²/2) = 1/2.

topics.quick_rule: Elastic: KE conserved, e = 1. Perfectly inelastic: maximum KE lost (consistent with momentum conservation), e = 0. All: total energy and momentum conserved.

grounding_summary: Page 261: Chapter 6 introduction. Work done by constant force W = Fs cosθ = F⃗·s⃗. Units: Joule (SI), erg (CGS). Dimension [ML²T⁻²].

Page 262: Positive work (F and displacement parallel), negative work (F and displacement anti-parallel), zero work (perpendicular, no displacement, or no force). Examples: coolie carrying load horizontally does zero work against gravity.
Page 263: Work done by variable force: dW = F⃗·ds⃗; W = ∫F·ds = area under F-x curve. Conservative field: W path-independent, closed-loop integral = 0. Work-energy theorem: W = ΔKE = ½m(v₂² − v₁²).
Page 264: Conservative (gravity) vs non-conservative (friction) forces. Frame-dependent work. Energy defined as capacity to do work. Units: J, erg, eV, kWh, cal. E = mc².
Page 265: Energy conversion table. Kinetic energy KE = ½mv². Derivation from v² = 2as. Calculus derivation dW = mv dv. KE always positive scalar.
Page 266: KE = P²/2m; P = √(2mE). Work-energy theorem W = ½m(v² − u²). Stopping distance x = mv²/2F; stopping time t = mv/F. Comparisons with same velocity, momentum, or KE.
Page 267: Graphs of KE vs various quantities. Stopping distance formulae with friction: x = v²/2μg. Potential energy concept introduced.
Page 268: Potential energy U = −∫F⃗·dr⃗; F = −dU/dx. 3D: F = −∇U. PE curve analysis; equilibrium types: stable (PE minimum, d²U/dx² > 0), unstable (PE maximum), neutral (PE constant).
Page 269: Spring constant k; spring PE U = ½kx² = ½Fx = F²/2k. Work done by spring table: natural→compressed = −½kx²; elongated→natural = +½kx². Energy graph for SHM showing KE and PE variation.
Page 270: Electrical PE = kq₁q₂/r. Gravitational PE U = mgh (near surface), U = −Gm₁m₂/r (general). Energy-height graph for projectile.
Page 271: Chain pulled off table: W = MgL/2n². Velocity of chain leaving table v = √(gL(1 − 1/n²)). Law of conservation of energy: KE + PE = constant. With friction: ΔE = W_friction.
Page 272: Power definitions: P_avg = W/t; P_inst = F⃗·v⃗. Units: Watt, hp (1 hp = 746 W), kW, MW. kWh = 3.6 × 10⁶ J. Slope of W-t graph = instantaneous power. Area under P-t graph = work.

Page 273: Collision introduction. Momentum always conserved in collision. KE conserved only in elastic. Types: perfectly elastic (e=1), inelastic (0<e<1), perfectly inelastic (e=0). 1D vs oblique collision.
Page 274: Perfectly elastic head-on: v₁ = (m₁−m₂)u₁/(m₁+m₂) + 2m₂u₂/(m₁+m₂); v₂ = (2m₁u₁ + (m₂−m₁)u₂)/(m₁+m₂). Newton's law of impact: relative velocity of separation = relative velocity of approach.

Page 275: Special cases of elastic collision. Equal masses (m₁=m₂): v₁=u₂, v₂=u₁ (exchange velocities). Heavy hits light (m₁>>m₂, u₂=0): v₁≈u₁, v₂=2u₁. Light hits heavy (m₁<<m₂, u₂=0): v₁≈−u₁, v₂≈0 (heavy unchanged).
Page 276: Fractional KE transfer: ΔK/K = 4m₁m₂/(m₁+m₂)². Maximum (100%) when m₁=m₂. Velocity/momentum/KE of stationary target table after elastic collision.
Page 277: Oblique elastic collision equations. Special case: m₁=m₂, u₂=0 → scattering angle θ+φ = 90°. Inelastic head-on collision formulas with coefficient of restitution e.

Page 278: Rebounding ball: h₁ = e²h₀; hₙ = e²ⁿh₀; total distance H = h₀(1+e²)/(1−e²); total time T = √(2h₀/g)(1+e)/(1−e). Perfectly inelastic: v_comb = (m₁u₁+m₂u₂)/(m₁+m₂); ΔKE = ½m₁m₂/(m₁+m₂)·(u₁−u₂)².
Page 279: Perfectly inelastic (opposite directions) case. Bullet-block ballistic pendulum: v_bullet derived from h reached. Loss in KE formula for bullet-block system.
Page 280: Tips and tricks. Key rules: KE ∝ P² (if mass constant), stopping distance ∝ v², engine power on incline P = (μcosθ + sinθ)mgv.
Page 146: Vertical circular motion theory. Velocity at angle θ: v = √(u² − 2gl(1−cosθ)). Tension: T = (m/l)[u² − gl(2−3cosθ)]. Table of v and T at positions A, B, C, D.
Page 147: Critical condition: u_min = √(5gl) at bottom; v at top = √(gl); T at top = 0. Table 3.5 critical values: T_A=6mg, T_B=3mg, T_C=0. Block on hemisphere loses contact at h = 2r/3; cosθ = 2/3.
grounding_excerpt: Work is said to be done when a force applied on the body displaces the body through a certain distance in the direction of force.
Thus work done by a force is equal to the scalar (or dot) product of the force and the displacement of the body.
The positive work signifies that the external force favours the motion of the body.
The negative work signifies that the external force opposes the motion of the body.
i.e. Area under force-displacement curve with proper algebraic sign represents work done by the force.
This is work energy theorem, it states that work done by a force acting on a body is equal to the change in the kinetic energy of the body.

A force is said to be non-conservative if work done by or against the force in moving a body from one position to another, depends on the path followed between these two positions and for complete cycle this work done can never be zero and there is change in kinetic energy over a round trip.
For an isolated system or body in presence of conservative forces, the sum of kinetic and potential energies at any point remains constant throughout the motion.
This work done is stored as the potential energy in the stretched spring.
kinetic energy is always positive scalar i.e. kinetic energy can never be negative.
Power of a body is defined as the rate at which the body can do the work.
i.e., power is equal to the scalar product of force with velocity.
In a collision, the effect of external forces such as gravity or friction are not taken into account as due to small duration of collision
If in a collision, kinetic energy after collision is equal to kinetic energy before collision, the collision is said to be perfectly elastic.
If in a collision two bodies stick together or move with same velocity after the collision, the collision is said to be perfectly inelastic.
Relative velocity of separation is equal to relative velocity of approach. This is called Newton's law of impact.
It means when two bodies of equal masses undergo head on elastic collision, their velocities get interchanged.
i.e. the ball rebounds with same speed in opposite direction when it collide with stationary and very massive wall.
In such types of collisions, the bodies move independently before collision but after collision as a one single body.
Transfer of kinetic energy will be maximum when the difference in masses is minimum
It means to complete the vertical circle the body must be projected with minimum velocity of $\sqrt{5g} \, l$ at the lowest point.
the block looses contact at the height of $\frac{2}{3} r$ from the ground.
K.E. of a body moving in horizontal circle is same throughout the path but the K.E. of the body moving in vertical circle is different at different places.

sources.title: Work, Energy, Power and Collision — Universal Book Depot Physics-1 (OCR)
sources.why_used: Primary OCR source text for chapter 6 theory, formulas, tables and examples (pages 261–280, 146–147).

sources.title: NEET Syllabus — Physics Chapter 6 Scope
sources.why_used: Verified syllabus scope: motion in vertical circle, elastic and inelastic collisions, conservation of mechanical energy, power.

key_facts: W = Fs cosθ; W is scalar; dimension [ML²T⁻²]
Conservative forces: gravity, spring, electrostatic — path-independent, ∮W = 0
U_spring = ½kx² (stored); W_by_spring = −½kx² (work done on block)
P_inst = F⃗·v⃗; 1 hp = 746 W; 1 kWh = 3.6 × 10⁶ J
e = v_separation/v_approach; elastic e=1; perfectly inelastic e=0
Equal masses elastic: exchange velocities (v1=u2, v2=u1)
Block on hemisphere loses contact at h = 2r/3

review.notes: All formulas, special cases, and numerical values verified against OCR pages 261–280 and 146–147. Vertical circular motion theory comes from pages 146–147 (Chapter 3 of this book) but is part of NEET Chapter 6 syllabus scope (confirmed on page 11 of the OCR: 'motion in a vertical circle, elastic and inelastic collisions in one and two dimensions'). Four topics created as specified. All grounding excerpts are verbatim from the OCR markdown files.
